name: Kaiparowits Plateau
short_description: Remote BLM plateau in southern Utah offering rockhounding for agate, jasper, and petrified wood across badlands and desert washes. Open public access with no permits required.
description:
The Kaiparowits Plateau is a sprawling high-elevation BLM landscape in Kane County, Utah, rising above 5,000 feet and featuring colorful badlands, desert washes, and exposed sedimentary layers. This remote area is prized by rockhounders for its abundance of agates, jasper nodules, chalcedony, and scattered pieces of petrified wood eroding from Upper Cretaceous and Tertiary formations.

The geology of Kaiparowits makes it an exceptional collecting destination. The plateau is underlain by the Kaiparowits Formation, a series of Late Cretaceous sandstones, siltstones, and mudstones deposited in ancient fluvial and lacustrine environments. Silica-rich groundwater percolating through these rocks over millions of years created the nodules and replacement minerals now exposed by erosion in washes, gullies, and hillside outcrops. The colorful banding in local agates and jasper reflects the iron oxide, manganese, and other trace elements concentrated during the mineral replacement process.

Collecting here is best done by walking washes after rainfall, scanning hillsides where erosion has freshly exposed material, and exploring the breaks and benches along ridge lines. The high elevation and remoteness mean weather changes quickly; afternoon thunderstorms can create flash-flooding hazards in narrow washes. Most collectors find material scattered across the surface rather than requiring significant digging.

Access is via rough dirt roads that require high-clearance vehicles and are impassable in wet conditions. The nearest services are in Kanab, roughly 40 miles away, so plan for a full day trip with supplies. This is genuine backcountry rockhounding - quiet, productive, and requiring self-sufficiency.
history: The Kaiparowits Plateau sits within the Colorado Plateau province where Late Cretaceous and Tertiary fluvial and lacustrine deposits accumulated in shallow basins. Silica-rich groundwater moving through these sedimentary sequences over tens of millions of years created the nodular agates and jasper now exposed by modern erosion - a process particularly active in the washes and badlands that characterize the plateau surface.
tips: Focus on collecting in dry washes and arroyos after recent rain, as fresh erosion exposes new material in banks and wash bottoms; look for semi-translucent nodules and banded pieces showing reds, browns, grays, and whites. Bring a portable classifier or mesh screen to sift smaller washes and concentrate material. The plateau breaks and benches adjacent to ridge lines often yield surface material without the digging required elsewhere - scan these areas with binoculars from a distance first. Watch afternoon skies closely; flash floods can fill washes quickly, so do not camp or collect in narrows during monsoon season.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,000 to 5,500 feet. Terrain is rough, broken badlands and desert plateau interspersed with eroded washes, gullies, and loose rock. Vegetation is sparse - mainly desert shrub and low vegetation. Walking distances from parking areas vary from 0.5 to 3+ miles depending on access point. Footing is uneven, loose, and rocky. No marked trails; navigation requires map and compass or GPS. High desert with poor soil and no water sources in the area.
best_season: April - June, September - October. Late spring and early fall offer moderate temperatures and lower risk of afternoon thunderstorms than monsoon season (July-August). Winter is possible but cold nights and potential snow close high-elevation roads. Summer heat (90F+) combined with afternoon lightning hazard in washes makes mid-summer collecting risky.
written_directions: From Kanab, take US 89 north for approximately 30 miles toward Paria. Turn east onto Skutumpah Road (rough dirt, high-clearance required) and travel 8 to 12 miles, depending on destination. Access points vary; consult a Kane County map or contact the Kanab BLM office for current road status and recommended parking areas. Roads are impassable when wet. No developed parking lots; pull-offs are informal dirt areas along the main road.
road_conditions: Very rough dirt roads with washboard, ruts, and rocks. High-clearance required; 4WD recommended. Roads impassable in rain or snow. Clearance 10+ inches minimum.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and dirt areas along Skutumpah Road and side roads. No developed parking lot. Space for 2-5 vehicles at most locations. No facilities, no water, no restrooms.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No use of explosives. Respect all natural and cultural resource protections. Pack out all trash. Do not damage natural features or disturb fossils beyond collecting loose material.
rules:

No motorized excavation
No explosives
Respect cultural resources
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Kanab, Utah - 40 miles west. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water. No services on the plateau itself.
accessibility_notes: Rough backcountry terrain unsuitable for limited mobility. High-clearance 4WD vehicle required; passenger cars will not reach productive collecting areas. Once parked, walking is over uneven, rocky, loose ground with no marked trails. Distances 0.5 to 3+ miles on foot from vehicle. Terrain slopes and breaks; footing is poor. Not wheelchair accessible. Requires strong fitness and backcountry navigation skills. Weather exposure is high with no shelter.

faq.question: What agates and jasper can I find rockhounding on the Kaiparowits Plateau?
faq.answer: The Kaiparowits Plateau yields colorful banded agates and jasper nodules in reds, browns, grays, and whites. Most material is small to medium-sized (golf-ball to fist-sized), with semi-translucent quality. Pieces often show intricate banding and patterns from iron oxide and silica deposition in ancient sedimentary layers. Petrified wood and chalcedony are also common surface finds.

faq.question: Is rockhounding on Kaiparowits Plateau free or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding on the Kaiparowits Plateau is free and requires no permit. It is BLM public land open to personal use collecting under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). You are limited to 25 lbs per day and cannot sell material or use motorized equipment. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What roads and vehicle type do I need to reach Kaiparowits Plateau?
faq.answer: Access is via Skutumpah Road, a rough dirt road east of Kanab on US 89. A high-clearance vehicle is required, and 4WD is strongly recommended. Standard passenger cars will not reach productive collecting areas. Roads are impassable in wet conditions. Current road status should be checked with the Kanab BLM office before traveling.

faq.question: When is the best time to rockhound at Kaiparowits Plateau?
faq.answer: The best collecting seasons are April through June and September through October. Late spring and early fall offer moderate temperatures and stable weather. Avoid mid-summer (July-August) due to afternoon thunderstorms and flash-flood hazard in washes. Winter is possible but roads may close from snow. Always check weather before heading out.

faq.question: Is Kaiparowits Plateau rockhounding suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: No, Kaiparowits Plateau is an expert-level rockhounding destination. It requires high-clearance 4WD vehicle, backcountry navigation skills, and self-sufficiency (it is 40 miles from town with no services). Terrain is rough and remote with hazards including flash floods, extreme heat, and wildlife. Beginners should start at fee dig sites or developed areas closer to Kanab.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding on Kaiparowits Plateau?
faq.answer: Bring a hammer, chisel, and small pick for careful collection. A portable mesh screen or classifier helps process wash material. Carry plenty of water (2+ gallons per person), sun protection, and a first aid kit. A topographic map, compass, and GPS device are essential for navigation. Bring full day supplies including food and extra fuel - the nearest gas station is in Kanab, 40 miles away.

meta_description: Free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and petrified wood on Utah's remote Kaiparowits Plateau. BLM land, 4WD required. Collect 25 lbs/day, no permit needed.
